pairs.a_text: Alex Hormozi argues that spreading founder attention across adjacent business models (a gym alongside a dietitian practice, a training university, a licensing model) causes the core vehicle to slow and the adjacent ventures to fail because neither receives the focused execution required for traction. He treats focus through subtraction as the mechanism that unlocks velocity in the core vehicle.
pairs.b_text: Alex Hormozi argues that a founder who plans to open a second location and have their newly hired GM simultaneously train a replacement at location one — without a sequenced, staged handoff — is likely to end up with neither location fully staffed, because the GM's attention is split before they have achieved full operating autonomy at location one.
